nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and semi-sheltered seaward reefs; juveniles often found among Acropora corals. Depth range typically 1–50 m, with records to 280 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented eating algae, small invertebrates, mollusks, sponges, polychaetes, and crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, newly introduced individuals tend to accept live brine shrimp or bloodworms initially; once acclimated, the diet can typically be transitioned to chopped squid, clams, mussels, and frozen herbivore preparations.

goodWith: fish too large to be threatened

summary: A striking boxfish native to Indo-Pacific reefs, notable for its bright yellow juvenile coloration with brown spots and a highly toxic skin secretion (ostracitoxin) that can prove lethal to tankmates under stress. Generally considered a species for experienced to expert-level aquarists only.
careNotes:
The Cubicus Boxfish is widely considered suitable only for very experienced aquarists. Its most critical trait is its ability to release ostracitoxin from mucous glands when stressed or dying — a toxin that has been reported to kill all other fish in a closed system within a very short period. Extreme care should be taken to minimize stress during handling, transport, and acclimation.

Juveniles display vivid yellow bodies with brown spots; adults tend to fade to brown with yellow-spotted patterning. Wild individuals inhabit lagoon and semi-sheltered seaward reefs across a broad Indo-Pacific range including the Red Sea, East Africa, Hawaii, and south to Lord Howe Island.

Sources disagree on minimum tank volume: LiveAquaria (expert page) recommends 250 gallons (946 L), while LiveAquaria's standard page and AquariumDomain cite 125 gallons (473 L), and ReefApp suggests 800 L. The averaged figure (740 L / ~195 gallons) is used here; reviewers may wish to standardize to 800 L given the species' large adult size and toxin-dilution considerations.

Adult size maximum similarly varies across sources: FishBase and ReefApp cite 45 cm, AquariumDomain cites 18 inches (~45.7 cm), and LiveAquaria cites 20 inches (~50.8 cm). The averaged maximum is approximately 46.6 cm.

Temperature recommendations also span two ranges: hobby sources (LiveAquaria, AquariumDomain) suggest 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), while FishBase wild data indicates 24.5–29°C. The averaged range of approximately 23–26.7°C is used here.

In the wild, reproduction is reported to occur in harems of one male and 2–4 females; breeding in captivity has rarely been achieved. The species is not protogynous — males maintain harems over females rather than switching sex in female-first fashion.

This species will nip at tubeworms and is generally not considered safe in reef tanks with small invertebrates, shrimps, or crabs. It is benthopelagic and solitary in the wild.
breedingNotes: Very little success has been reported in breeding the Cubicus Boxfish in captivity. Wild populations are reported to reproduce in harems, with a single male presiding over two to four females.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (45 cm max) and temperature range (24.5–29°C wild data), taxonomy, native range, and habitat. FishBase temperature range is warmer than hobby-source recommendations; averaged across sources.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Additional cross-check on size (18 in / ~45.7 cm), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.020–1.025), tank size (125 gal / 473 L), and diet. Consistent with LiveAquaria standard page on most parameters.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Additional cross-check; cites max size 45 cm, minimum tank 800 L, salinity ~1.026. Tank size recommendation is notably higher than hobby sources; factored into averaged minimum volume.

fish.breedingNotes: Very little success has been reported in breeding the Cubicus Boxfish in captivity. Wild populations are reported to reproduce in harems, with a single male presiding over two to four females.
